skill.name: dast-nuclei
skill.description: Guides authorized dynamic application security testing with ProjectDiscovery Nuclei. It covers target scoping, template selection, authenticated scans, CI/CD examples, result validation, and reporting workflows.
skill.summary: Run authorized Nuclei vulnerability scans with scoped targets, templates, CI workflows, and validation guidance.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, environment, and script patterns. Review shows these are mostly expected for an authorized DAST/Nuclei security skill and its examples, with no evidence of hidden exfiltration or prompt injection. The skill should publish with warnings because users can run network scans, handle credentials, and copy one risky CI installer pattern.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Network Scanning Risk
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for network behavior. The skill instructs users to run Nuclei against URLs and APIs, which is expected for DAST but can affect third-party or production systems if scope and rate limits are wrong.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited instructions and workflow target live URLs. This is legitimate for a DAST skill, but the operational risk is real without authorization and throttling.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Is Core Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for command execution, with legitimate intent. The skill teaches users to run nuclei, Python helpers, and CI commands; these commands are visible examples rather than hidden execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are directly present and aligned with the stated scanning purpose. I found no evidence that the skill silently executes them without user action.

medium_findings.title: Credential And Sensitive Endpoint Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for credential and sensitive-data handling. The skill includes authenticated scan examples, environment-variable tokens, and templates for exposed configuration paths such as environment files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly intended for authorized testing and secret detection. They still require careful handling because scan output may contain credentials or sensitive endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-To-Shell Installer In CI Template
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for a risky CI pattern. The CI template includes a curl-to-bash installer for a security tool, which can execute remote code during a workflow if copied without pinning or verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present. It is in an optional CI template, so it is not proof of malicious intent, but it is a concrete supply-chain risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholders
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious network destinations. The hardcoded URLs are documentation examples, default staging targets, or upstream documentation links rather than covert endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are example targets or public security references. I found no evidence that data is sent to an author-controlled collection endpoint.

low_findings.title: Educational Vulnerable-Code Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for script injection and weak-pattern alerts. The XSS, SQL injection, weak crypto, and attack-technique mentions appear in educational examples and mappings for security detection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file context labels these snippets as vulnerable examples or framework mappings. They are not active exploit code invoked by the skill.

low_findings.title: GitHub Token Use Is Standard CI Context
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW for CI token exposure. The workflow references GitHub-provided tokens for actions such as SARIF upload and PR comments, which is normal but should remain least-privileged.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token references are visible in CI templates and not exfiltrated. Permission settings and copied workflows still need human review in each repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: A CI template installs a tool by piping a remote script into bash. Users should replace this with a pinned package, checksum verification, or a trusted action version.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This exact supply-chain pattern is present at the cited line. The risk depends on whether users copy the optional workflow into production CI.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Scans Pass Session Material To Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Authentication examples pass cookies or bearer tokens into Nuclei command invocations. This is useful for coverage, but scan logs and command history may expose sensitive material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly combine credentials with scan commands. This is normal for authenticated DAST but requires strict secret handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive File Exposure Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The custom template examples include checks for exposed configuration and secret-like values. This can reveal real secrets in authorized scans and must be handled as sensitive output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples intentionally look for secret exposure. The intent is defensive, but the output can contain sensitive data.

content.value_statement: Security teams need fast checks for known vulnerabilities without building every test from scratch. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide authorized Nuclei scans, CI workflows, and result validation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Nuclei installation, template updates, and basic scan commands.
Guides target scoping, severity filtering, rate limits, and concurrency settings.
Provides authenticated scan patterns for bearer tokens, cookies, OAuth, SAML, and MFA workflows.
Includes CI/CD workflow examples for GitHub Actions and broader security pipelines.
Maps scan review steps to OWASP, CWE, false-positive handling, and remediation reporting.
Shows how to design custom Nuclei-style templates for organization-specific checks.
limitations: It does not grant authorization to scan third-party systems.
It depends on Nuclei and related tools being installed by the user.
It cannot confirm every finding without manual validation and application context.
Authenticated scans may expose secrets in logs if users do not protect outputs.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Vulnerability Check
use_cases.description: Run scoped Nuclei scans against staging applications before release and triage critical findings.

use_cases.title: Security Gate In CI
use_cases.description: Add Nuclei scan jobs to CI pipelines with severity thresholds and artifact reporting.

use_cases.title: Custom Exposure Detection
use_cases.description: Create and validate custom templates for internal exposure patterns and recurring misconfigurations.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Basic Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized Nuclei scan for a staging web application. Include scope, severity filters, rate limits, and output handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Scan Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Nuclei findings and help me group them by severity, likely false positives, verification steps, and remediation owners.

prompt_templates.title: Build A CI Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub Actions workflow for authorized Nuclei scanning that uploads reports, fails on critical findings, and protects secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Design Custom Templates
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design custom Nuclei templates for our internal API exposure patterns, including matchers, severity, references, and safe validation steps.

output_examples.input: Plan a cautious scan for a production API.
output_examples.output: A scoped scan plan with written authorization checks, low rate limits, critical-only templates, credential handling rules, and rollback contacts.

output_examples.input: Summarize Nuclei results for a release review.
output: High findings grouped by affected service.
Likely false positives with evidence to check.

output_examples.input: Create guidance for an authenticated scan.
output_examples.output: A workflow that uses short-lived test credentials, avoids logging secrets, limits templates, and records authorization and scope.

best_practices: Get written authorization and confirm scope before any network scan.
Use conservative rate limits for production targets and increase only after monitoring impact.
Treat scan outputs, authenticated headers, and extracted values as sensitive data.
anti_patterns: Scanning third-party assets without explicit permission.
Copying CI installer commands without pinning versions or verifying sources.
Raising incidents from Nuclei output without manual validation.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps plan and operate authorized Nuclei-based dynamic security scans for web applications, APIs, and infrastructure.

faq.question: Can it run scans by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must run Nuclei and related commands in their own environment.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can be used for production only with authorization, conservative rate limits, monitoring, and clear rollback contacts.

faq.question: Does it support authenticated scanning?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents header, cookie, OAuth, SAML, and MFA patterns, but users must protect all credentials and outputs.

faq.question: How should false positives be handled?
faq.answer: Verify critical and high findings manually, compare evidence against application behavior, and document the reason for any dismissal.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is dual-use security guidance with external commands, network scanning, credential handling, and a risky CI installer example.
